stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman at 35+4 weeks presents to the birth suite with frontal headache, blurred vision and right upper quadrant pain. Her booking BP was 110/68. She has had no prior medical conditions and no other proteinuria during this pregnancy. She is not in labour and the CTG is reactive with no decelerations.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 178/112 (repeat 175/108 after 15 minutes), HR 92, RR 16, SpO2 99% RA, T 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Brisk reflexes with 2 beats of clonus, no papilloedema, fundal height 35 cm, foetus moving, no abdominal tenderness on palpation
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis 3+ protein, urine protein-creatinine ratio 75 mg/mmol; Hb 124, plt 142 x10^9/L, ALT 88 U/L, urate 0.42, creatinine 78 micromol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management of her blood pressure?

options.text: Commence oral labetalol 200 mg three times daily and reassess in 4 hours
options.rationale: Oral therapy cannot achieve sufficient acute control in severe hypertension over 170/110; intravenous therapy is required. (Source: SOMANZ pre-eclampsia 2014)

options.text: Commence oral methyldopa 250 mg three times daily and observe maternal status overnight
options.rationale: Methyldopa is for mild hypertension long-term; onset is too slow for severe acute hypertension. (Source: SOMANZ 2014)

options.text: Give sublingual nifedipine 10 mg immediately to lower the blood pressure rapidly
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine causes rapid uncontrolled hypotension and is contraindicated in pregnancy hypertension; oral immediate-release nifedipine is acceptable but sublingual is not. (Source: SOMANZ 2014)

options.text: Start intravenous hydralazine 20 mg bolus and review effect at 30 minutes
options.rationale: Hydralazine bolus of 20 mg is excessive; AU dose is 5 mg slow IV with redose if needed. The labetalol route is preferred unless contraindicated. (Source: SOMANZ 2014)

options.text: Administer intravenous labetalol 20 mg bolus and titrate up to 80 mg as required
options.rationale: Severe hypertension (BP over 170 systolic or 110 diastolic) needs rapid lowering to prevent stroke; AU first-line is IV labetalol 20 mg bolus repeated and doubled to maximum 80 mg per dose (cumulative 220 mg). (Source: SOMANZ pre-eclampsia 2014; eTG)

explanation.summary: Severe hypertension in pre-eclampsia is BP at or above 170 systolic or 110 diastolic and is a medical emergency due to stroke risk. Australian SOMANZ first-line is IV labetalol 20 mg bolus (doubled every 10 to 15 minutes to 40, 80 mg per dose; cumulative max 220 mg). Alternatives are IV hydralazine 5 mg slow bolus or oral immediate-release nifedipine 10 mg. Target is gradual reduction to systolic 140 to 160 and diastolic 90 to 100 within 1 hour; faster reduction risks foetoplacental hypoperfusion. Concurrent magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis if features of severe disease.
key_points: Severe HTN = BP at or above 170/110 - urgent acute treatment.
First-line: IV labetalol 20 mg bolus; titrate 40, 80 mg.
Alternatives: hydralazine 5 mg slow IV; oral immediate-release nifedipine 10 mg.
Avoid sublingual nifedipine; avoid ACEi/ARB in pregnancy.
Target BP 140 to 160/90 to 100 within 1 hour; faster lowering risks placental hypoperfusion.
explanation.pearls: Magnesium sulfate (4 g loading IV over 20 minutes then 1 g/h infusion) is indicated for seizure prophylaxis in severe pre-eclampsia regardless of BP control; continue 24 hours after delivery or last seizure. Monitor patellar reflexes, RR, and urine output for toxicity.

stem: A 32-year-old G3P3 woman has just had a spontaneous vaginal birth of a 4250 g infant after a 14-hour labour augmented with oxytocin. The placenta has been delivered intact 10 minutes ago. Estimated blood loss to date is 900 mL with ongoing brisk vaginal bleeding. The midwife is preparing to rub up the fundus while the registrar performs assessment. The patient has no known drug allergies and no hypertension.

data_table.Examination: Boggy uterus palpated at the umbilicus, no perineal laceration deeper than 1st degree, placenta intact on inspection, no retained products visible on speculum
data_table.Investigations: Hb 96 (predelivery 132), platelets 184 x10^9/L; fibrinogen 3.6 g/L; group A positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological management?

options.text: Give intravenous oxytocin 5 IU slow bolus then 40 IU infusion in 1 L normal saline
options.rationale: AU first-line for atonic PPH after fundal rub is IV oxytocin 5 IU bolus followed by 40 IU in 1 L crystalloid infused over 4 hours; address atony before considering second-line uterotonics. (Source: RANZCOG PPH 2023)

options.text: Administer intramuscular ergometrine 250 mcg into the deltoid muscle now
options.rationale: Ergometrine is second-line uterotonic and is contraindicated in hypertension; oxytocin is AU first-line in atonic PPH. (Source: RANZCOG PPH 2023)

options.text: Insert a Bakri intrauterine balloon and inflate it to 500 mL with sterile saline
options.rationale: Balloon tamponade is considered when first-line uterotonics fail; not the immediate first step in atonic PPH. (Source: RANZCOG PPH 2023)

options.text: Administer intramuscular carboprost 250 mcg into the deltoid muscle every 15 minutes
options.rationale: Carboprost is reserved for atonic PPH unresponsive to oxytocin and ergometrine; not first-line. (Source: RANZCOG PPH 2023)

options.text: Commence tranexamic acid 1 g IV as the sole pharmacological intervention
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid is an adjunct to uterotonics (give within 3 hours of bleeding onset), not a substitute for uterotonics in atony. (Source: WOMAN trial 2017)

explanation.summary: Atonic postpartum haemorrhage accounts for around 70 percent of primary PPH and is treated by mechanical measures (fundal rub, bladder empty, bimanual compression) plus uterotonics in sequence: oxytocin first-line (5 IU IV slow bolus then 40 IU in 1 L over 4 hours), ergometrine 250 mcg IM (avoid if hypertensive), carboprost 250 mcg IM every 15 minutes (max 8 doses, avoid in asthma), misoprostol 800 mcg sublingual. Tranexamic acid 1 g IV is given within 3 hours of bleeding onset and repeated if ongoing. Mechanical measures escalate to balloon tamponade and surgical management if uterotonics fail.
key_points: 4 Ts: Tone (70 percent), Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin.
First-line atonic: oxytocin IV 5 IU + 40 IU infusion + fundal rub + empty bladder.
Second-line: ergometrine 250 mcg IM (not in hypertension); carboprost 250 mcg IM (not in asthma).
Adjunct: tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours of bleeding.
Failure: balloon tamponade, B-Lynch, internal iliac ligation, hysterectomy.
explanation.pearls: WOMAN trial showed tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours of bleeding onset reduces death due to bleeding from PPH by 31 percent; benefit is lost if given after 3 hours - record bleeding-onset time precisely.

stem: A 23-year-old nulliparous woman with a 3-year history of irregular periods (2 to 4 cycles per year), acne and mild hirsutism is reviewed by her GP. Pelvic ultrasound shows multifollicular ovaries with the right ovary measuring 11 mL volume. Her BMI is 28, fasting glucose 5.0 mmol/L, lipids normal, smoke-free, normotensive. She is currently sexually active with her long-term partner and using condoms. She is not currently planning pregnancy.

data_table.Examination: Mild acne on face and back, terminal hairs on chin and lower abdomen (modified Ferriman-Gallwey 9), pelvic examination unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: Serum total testosterone 2.4 nmol/L, free androgen index 7.8, SHBG 32 nmol/L, LH:FSH 2.4, fasting glucose 5.0, HbA1c 35 mmol/mol, lipids within range

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?

options.text: Start metformin 500 mg twice daily and review menstrual cycle in 3 months
options.rationale: Metformin is considered for insulin resistance or metabolic features but is not first-line for irregular cycles and androgen symptoms when contraception is needed. (Source: International PCOS guideline 2023)

options.text: Recommend lifestyle change alone and review symptoms and cycle in 6 months
options.rationale: Lifestyle is foundational but does not adequately treat irregular cycles or contraception need; combine with hormonal management. (Source: International PCOS guideline 2023)

options.text: Prescribe combined oral contraceptive pill 30 mcg ethinyloestradiol with drospirenone
options.rationale: First-line for irregular menses, androgen symptoms, contraception in PCOS without contraindications is COCP; drospirenone has mild antiandrogen effect for hirsutism. (Source: International PCOS guideline 2023; RACGP)

options.text: Refer urgently to reproductive endocrinology for ovulation induction with letrozole
options.rationale: Ovulation induction is for fertility planning, not contraceptive use; she is not currently seeking pregnancy. (Source: International PCOS guideline 2023)

options.text: Insert a levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system for cycle control and contraception
options.rationale: LNG-IUS controls bleeding and provides contraception but does not improve hirsutism or androgenic acne; COCP is preferred when androgen symptoms are present. (Source: International PCOS guideline 2023)

explanation.summary: PCOS in women not seeking pregnancy and without contraindications is managed first-line with COCP for irregular cycles, contraception and androgen symptoms (acne, hirsutism). The 2023 International PCOS guideline supports any COCP formulation; preparations with drospirenone, cyproterone or dienogest have mild antiandrogen effects. Cyproterone-containing COCPs have higher VTE risk and are not first-line. Lifestyle changes (5 to 10 percent weight loss) improve insulin resistance and ovulation but supplement rather than replace pharmacotherapy. Metformin is added for metabolic features or to support lifestyle if BMI is elevated.
key_points: First-line: COCP for cycle regulation + androgen symptoms + contraception.
Lifestyle: 5 to 10 percent weight loss improves all PCOS features.
Add metformin for insulin resistance, BMI over 30, or impaired fasting glucose.
Cyproterone-containing COCP NOT first-line (VTE risk).
Endometrial protection: regular progestogen-induced bleed if not on COCP.
explanation.pearls: Women with PCOS not on cyclical hormonal therapy need endometrial protection with at least 4 cycles per year via cyclical progestogen (medroxyprogesterone 10 mg daily for 14 days every 3 months) to reduce endometrial hyperplasia risk.

stem: A 22-year-old woman attends her GP with a 4-year history of progressively worsening cyclical pelvic pain. Her periods began at age 12 and have always been heavy. Pain peaks on day 1 of menses and is now interfering with university attendance. She has tried regular ibuprofen with limited benefit. Her cervical screening is up to date, sexually active with one partner, using condoms.

data_table.Examination: Bimanual pelvic examination: uterosacral nodularity, mild tenderness, no adnexal mass; cervix appears healthy on speculum examination
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative, transvaginal ultrasound: no endometrioma seen, uterus normal size, ovaries normal volume

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Refer urgently to gynaecology for diagnostic laparoscopy and definitive histology
options.rationale: Laparoscopy is reserved for failure of medical therapy or for women seeking immediate fertility; first-line is empirical treatment of clinical endometriosis. (Source: RANZCOG endometriosis 2021)

options.text: Refer for surgical excision of endometriotic lesions despite normal ultrasound findings
options.rationale: Surgical excision is for failure of medical therapy, deep infiltrating disease, or fertility considerations; first-line is medical. (Source: RANZCOG 2021)

options.text: Commence GnRH agonist therapy with goserelin 3.6 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks
options.rationale: GnRH analogues are reserved for failure of first-line treatments; limited to 6 months due to bone loss without add-back. (Source: RANZCOG 2021)

options.text: Trial combined oral contraceptive pill in extended (continuous) regimen for 3 cycles
options.rationale: First-line for suspected endometriosis without contraindications is COCP continuous (skipping pill-free interval) plus regular NSAIDs; review at 3 to 6 months. (Source: RANZCOG endometriosis 2021)

options.text: Recommend pelvic floor physiotherapy alone for 6 months before considering further investigation
options.rationale: Physiotherapy is helpful adjunct but not adequate alone for moderate cyclical pelvic pain with examination findings; medical therapy comes first. (Source: RANZCOG 2021)

explanation.summary: Suspected endometriosis is now treated empirically based on clinical features without requiring laparoscopic confirmation. First-line management is combined hormonal contraception (COCP continuous, vaginal ring, patch) or progestogens (norethisterone, dienogest, LNG-IUS), plus regular NSAIDs at menstruation. Continuous COCP for 3 cycles followed by 4-day break is the most evidence-supported regimen. Review at 3 to 6 months; failure escalates to alternative hormonal therapy, surgical referral, or GnRH analogue with add-back. Laparoscopy is reserved for treatment failure, fertility concerns, or deep infiltrating disease.
key_points: First-line: continuous COCP or LNG-IUS + regular NSAIDs.
Empirical treatment without laparoscopic diagnosis.
Second-line: progestogen (dienogest 2 mg daily) or alternative hormonal contraception.
GnRH analogues for treatment failure (max 6 months without add-back).
Surgical referral for failure, deep disease, endometrioma over 4 cm, or fertility issues.
explanation.pearls: Women with endometriosis on continuous COCP may experience breakthrough bleeding at 6 to 12 months; a 4-day pill-free interval re-establishes cycle suppression - the 4-day break is shorter than the standard 7 days to minimise oestrogen withdrawal.

stem: A 42-year-old G2P2 woman attends her GP with 18 months of progressively heavier periods. She changes super-plus tampons every 2 hours, passes clots and has had 2 episodes of flooding through clothing. Periods are regular every 26 days lasting 7 days. She is up to date with cervical screening (HPV negative 8 months ago), pelvic ultrasound shows normal endometrium and small intramural fibroid 12 mm. She does not want more children.

data_table.Examination: Pallor of conjunctivae, abdomen soft, bulky uterus to 12 weeks size, no adnexal masses palpated, cervix appears healthy
data_table.Investigations: Hb 98 g/L, MCV 78 fL, ferritin 14 mcg/L, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, coagulation normal, beta-hCG negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?

options.text: Insert a levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system 52 mg and start oral iron replacement
options.rationale: LNG-IUS 52 mg is AU and NICE first-line for HMB without structural cause or with minor fibroids (under 3 cm), giving 70 to 95 percent reduction in blood loss; concurrent iron replacement for documented deficiency. (Source: RANZCOG HMB 2021; NICE NG88)

options.text: Refer for endometrial ablation as a same-week outpatient procedure under anaesthesia
options.rationale: Endometrial ablation is reserved for failure or contraindication to medical therapy and after structural causes have been managed. (Source: NICE NG88 heavy menstrual bleeding)

options.text: Prescribe tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily for the duration of each menstrual bleed
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid is effective but not preferred first-line when LNG-IUS is acceptable; LNG-IUS gives larger sustained reduction and provides contraception. (Source: RANZCOG 2021)

options.text: Trial mefenamic acid 500 mg three times daily during menstruation only
options.rationale: NSAIDs reduce bleeding by around 20 to 50 percent; less effective than LNG-IUS and not preferred first-line when contraception is also desired. (Source: RANZCOG 2021)

options.text: Refer for hysterectomy after 6 months of failed medical therapy as the only definitive option
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is definitive but should follow failed medical therapy; LNG-IUS trial precedes surgical referral. (Source: RANZCOG 2021)

explanation.summary: Heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) affects up to 1 in 4 women and is defined by impact on quality of life rather than absolute blood loss. First-line medical management without structural cause or with minor fibroids is the levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS) 52 mg, achieving 70 to 95 percent reduction in blood loss over 6 to 12 months. Alternatives include tranexamic acid 1 g three or four times daily during bleeding, mefenamic acid 500 mg three times daily, COCP continuous, or oral progestogens. Iron deficiency is treated concurrently. Endometrial ablation and hysterectomy are reserved for failure of medical therapy.
key_points: LNG-IUS first-line: 70 to 95 percent reduction, contraception included.
Tranexamic acid 1 g qid during bleeding: 50 percent reduction.
Mefenamic acid: 20 to 50 percent reduction, also helps dysmenorrhoea.
Treat iron deficiency concurrently - 60 percent of HMB have low ferritin.
Refer to gynaecology for failure of 3 months medical therapy, fibroids over 3 cm, suspected pathology.
explanation.pearls: Always check for von Willebrand disease in adolescents with menarche-onset HMB; prevalence is 13 percent in this group and detection changes management options (DDAVP, factor concentrates).
